plainMeaning: This verse begins naming Mahāvīra's eleven chief disciples: first Indrabhūti, then Agnibhūti, third Vāyubhūti, then Vyakta and Sudharman.
recitation: paḍham' ettha indabhūi, bīe puṇa hoi aggibhūi tti | taïe ya vāubhūi, tao viyatte suhamme ya ||

wordByWord.etymology: sandhi of paḍhama (Skt. prathama, 'first') + ettha (Skt. atra, 'here/in this')
wordByWord.notes: The ordinal sets up the enumerative scaffold paḍhama–bīa–taïa that structures the whole gāthā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit for Skt. Indrabhūti; the eldest of the three Brahmin brothers, of the Gautama gotra
wordByWord.notes: Mahāvīra's foremost disciple, hailed elsewhere simply as Gautama Svāmī.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bīa (Skt. dvitīya, 'second'), in the ordinal/locative form bīe

wordByWord.notes: A connective particle, here metrical filler advancing the list.

wordByWord.etymology: aggibhūi (Skt. Agnibhūti) + tti (Skt. iti), the quotative marking the name 'thus called'
wordByWord.notes: The tti ('iti') functions as a naming-marker; Agnibhūti is the second brother and second Gaṇadhara.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit taïa (Skt. tṛtīya, 'third'), ordinal form taïe
Prakrit for Skt. Vāyubhūti; the youngest of the three Gautama brothers
Prakrit for Skt. Vyakta; nominative in the metrical -e form
Prakrit for Skt. Sudharman; nominative in the metrical -e form
wordByWord.notes: The sole Gaṇadhara who long outlived Mahāvīra and through whom the surviving Āgamic canon was transmitted.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ya (Skt. ca, 'and'), closing the pair Vyakta-and-Sudharman

literalTranslation: First here is Indrabhūti; the second, moreover, is the one called Agnibhūti; and the third is Vāyubhūti; thereafter Vyakta and Sudharman.
idiomaticTranslation: Foremost among them stands Indrabhūti; next comes the one named Agnibhūti; the third is Vāyubhūti; and after them, Vyakta and Sudharman.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having saluted all twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras in the preceding verses, the hymn now turns from the Jinas to the human bearers of Vīra's teaching and begins, name by name, the roll-call of his eleven Gaṇadharas. This verse delivers the first five. The poet builds a deliberately bare mnemonic frame — paḍhama ('first'), bīa ('second'), taïa ('third'), then tao ('thereafter') — onto which the names are hung, so the reciter fixes both order and identity in one breath. Notice the cluster Indrabhūti–Agnibhūti–Vāyubhūti: tradition holds these three to be Brahmin brothers of the Gautama gotra, learned Vedic ritualists who came to dispute Mahāvīra and instead became his disciples, each surrendering a private doubt (about the existence of the soul, of karma, of the identity of body and soul). Their shared '-bhūti' suffix and birth-order are exactly what the ordinals preserve. The little tti after Agnibhūti is not idle: it is the quotative iti, marking the syllables as a proper name being formally registered. The verse then names Vyakta and binds Sudharman to him with the conjunctive ya — a fitting closure, since Sudharman is the Gaṇadhara of greatest consequence for everyone reciting this very text. The list is left open: it spills deliberately into the next gāthā, which supplies the remaining six and the refrain 'these are the Gaṇadharas of Vīra.'
elaboration.doctrinalContext: A Gaṇadhara is not merely a senior monk but the disciple who hears the Tīrthaṅkara's three seed-utterances (tri-padī: 'it arises, it perishes, it persists') and from them composes the twelve-limbed canon (dvādaśāṅgī). This verse therefore names the very channel by which arhat-revelation became transmissible śruta — apt within the Nandī-sūtra, whose theme is knowledge (jñāna) itself. Sudharman's mention carries special doctrinal weight: he alone among the eleven survived long after Mahāvīra's nirvāṇa, and the Śvetāmbara Āgamas are framed as his teaching to his pupil Jambū ('thus have I heard'). To name him here is to point at the root of the recited scripture in one's own hands.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the practitioner honors the human lineage that preserved the path — gratitude not only to the Jina but to the disciples without whom no sūtra would have reached us. It invites the reflection that learned doubt, honestly surrendered before a true teacher, becomes the seed of liberating knowledge.

crossReferences.text: Sthavirāvalī (lineage section) naming the eleven Gaṇadharas of Mahāvīra
Gaṇadharavāda — the eleven Gaṇadharas and their resolved doubts

sources.title: Gautama Swami (Indrabhūti Gautama)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Indrabhūti as Mahāvīra's first Gaṇadhara and eldest of the three Gautama brothers

sources.title: Ganadhara
sources.consultedFor: Definition and roll-call of Mahāvīra's eleven Gaṇadharas; their composition of the dvādaśāṅgī from the tri-padī

sources.title: Sudharmaswami
sources.consultedFor: Sudharman as the surviving Gaṇadhara through whom the Śvetāmbara canon was transmitted to Jambū

sources.title: Nandī-sūtra (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Source-text context of the Gaṇadhara enumeration in the Nandī opening

notes: The five names and their order (Indrabhūti, Agnibhūti, Vāyubhūti, Vyakta, Sudharman) are uniformly attested across Śvetāmbara sources; this gāthā gives the first five of eleven and runs on into v.23, which completes the list and adds the refrain 'gaṇaharā hunti vīrassa.' The -e endings of viyatte/suhamme are nominatives in the verse's metrical register, not locatives. paḍhamettha is read as paḍhama+ettha by sandhi; tti is the quotative iti marking the name Agnibhūti. Per the book's orthography rules, mātrās, anusvāras and conjuncts follow the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha printing verbatim and are not normalized.
